en.extract:
The black-capped piprites (Piprites pileata), also known as the black-capped manakin, bailarín castaño, or piprites capirotado, is a species of suboscine passerine. It has been placed in the genus Piprites, part of the Tyrannidae family. Previously, the species was included in the family Pipridae, but was reclassified following genetic studies.
It is found in the Atlantic Forest, especially in regions where Araucaria angustifolia are found, in the highlands of southeastern Brazil and northeastern Argentina. Until the recent rediscovery in Argentina, the only confirmed record there was a specimen taken in 1959. It is considered as rare and its population is in decline; it is considered vulnerable by BirdLife International. It is known from a number of protected areas, including the Itatiaia National Park in Rio de Janeiro, and Campos do Jordão State Park in São Paulo.


Distribution and habitat
The black-capped piprites is distributed unevenly in the southeastern region of Brazil (south of Minas Gerais and the state of Rio de Janeiro) and in the extreme northeast of Argentina, in the province of Misiones.
The species is found in the canopy and subcanopy around the edge of the Atlantic Forest, especially in regions where Brazilian pines are found. It is found at altitudes from 500 to 2,000 meters.


Description
The adult black-capped piprites is a tyranid with a black cap and yellow beak. Its length has been measured to range from 12–12.5 centimetres (4.7–4.9 in). It has contrasting chestnut-colored upperparts, yellow legs, blackish and yellow-green remiges. In males, primary feathers are notably whitish. Females resemble males, though they are drab olive colored above rather than chestnut. The juvenile has not been described.


Diet
Black-capped piprites feed on small fruits and arthropods. They have been recorded as feeding in the canopy, subcanopy and understorey of Chusquea bamboo thickets.


Reproduction
The male black-capped piprites has a display ritual it performs during the mating season. Little else is known about their reproduction. The mating season takes place between September and November; nest construction has been observed in October.


Vocalizations
The species' calls include a single "whééu" sound, followed by a variable series of brief, softer vocalizations.


Conservation
The black-capped piprites is classified as near-threatened by the International Union for Conservation of Nature, though it was classified as vulnerable in 2016, due to its small range and fragmented and declining population. In 2020, its total population was estimated at 2,800 to 22,400 mature individuals. Its range is limited by the presence of Brazilian pines; recommendations have been made to remediate the declining Brazilian pine population in order to support the black-capped piprites species.


Systematics


Original description
The species P. pileatus was first described in 1822 by Dutch zoologist Coenraad Jacob Temminck; its type locality is Curitiba, Paraná, Brazil.


Etymology
The generic name Piprites comes from the Greek πιπρα (pipra) or πιπρω (piprō): a small bird mentioned by Aristotle and other contemporary authors but never identified, associated with neotropical manakins in the genus Pipra, and ιτης (itēs): "similar to", meaning in total "similar to Pipra". The species name pileata comes from the Latin pileatus, meaning "capped".


Taxonomy
The black-capped piprites is monotypic. Its relation to the genus Piprites and other tyranids is uncertain. Molecular genetic studies in the early 2000s demonstrated relationships with Rhynchocyclidae and Tyrannidae. Following these studies, the family Pipritidae was proposed in 2013 with Piprites as the sole genus. The family name was adopted by Avibase and the Brazilian Committee for Ornithological Records, however, the South American Classification Committee (SACC) maintained the genus as incertae sedis and rejected the family name recommendation in 2016. 3 years later, the SACC approved Pipritinae as a subfamily of Tyrannidae.


References


External links
BirdLife species factsheet
Photo of a male black-capped piprites – Jquental.

The '''black-capped piprites''' ('''''Piprites pileata'''''), also known as the '''black-capped manakin''', '''bailarín castaño''',<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal |last=Bodrati |first=Alejandro |last2=Maders |first2=Claudio |last3=Di Santo |first3=Gastón |last4=Cockle |first4=Kristina |last5=Ignacio Areta |first5=Juan |last6=Manuel Segovia |first6=José |date=2009 |title=Distribución, hábitat, y historia natural del Bailarín Castaño Piprites pileata, una especie Críticamente Amenazada en Argentina |trans-title=Distribution, habitat, and natural history of the Bailarín Castaño Piprites pileata, a critically threatened species in Argentina |url=https://www.researchgate.net/publication/250613369_Distribucion_habitat_e_historia_natural_del_Bailarin_Castano_Piprites_pileata_una_especie_Criticamente_Amenazada_en_Argentina |journal=[[Cotinga]] |language=es |volume=31}}</ref> or '''piprites capirotado''',<ref>{{cite journal|access-date=20 August 2024 |author=Bernis, F; De Juana, E; Del Hoyo, J; Fernández-Cruz, M; Ferrer, X; Sáez-Royuela, R; Sargatal, J |date=2004 |issn=0570-7358 |location=Madrid |number=2 |pages=491-499 |periodical=[[Ardeola (revista)|Ardeola]] |publisher=[[Sociedad Española de Ornitología|SEO/BirdLife]] |series=''[[Aves del Mundo|Handbook of the Birds of the World]]'' |title=Nombres en castellano de las aves del mundo recomendados por la Sociedad Española de Ornitología (Novena parte: Orden Passeriformes, Familias  Cotingidae a Motacillidae) |url=https://www.ardeola.org/uploads/articles/docs/1213.pdf |volume=51}}<!-- auto-translated from Spanish by Module:CS1 translator --></ref> is a species of [[suboscine]] [[passerine]]. It has been placed in the genus ''[[Piprites]]'', part of the [[Tyrannidae]] family. Previously, the species was included in the family [[Pipridae]], but was reclassified following genetic studies.<ref name=":1">{{Cite journal |last=Tello |first=Jose G. |last2=Moyle |first2=Robert G. |last3=Marchese |first3=Daniel J. |last4=Cracraft |first4=Joel |date=October 2009 |title=Phylogeny and phylogenetic classification of the tyrant flycatchers, cotingas, manakins, and their allies (Aves: Tyrannides) |url=https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/j.1096-0031.2009.00254.x |journal=Cladistics |language=en |volume=25 |issue=5 |pages=429–467 |doi=10.1111/j.1096-0031.2009.00254.x |issn=0748-3007|url-access=subscription }}</ref>

It is found in the [[Atlantic Forest]], especially in regions where ''[[Araucaria angustifolia]]'' are found, in the highlands of southeastern [[Brazil]] and northeastern [[Argentina]]. Until the recent rediscovery in Argentina, the only confirmed record there was a [[Biological specimen|specimen]] taken in 1959. It is considered as rare and its population is in decline; it is considered [[Vulnerable species|vulnerable]] by [[BirdLife International]]. It is known from a number of protected areas, including the [[Itatiaia National Park]] in [[Rio de Janeiro (state)|Rio de Janeiro]], and [[Campos do Jordão State Park]] in [[São Paulo (state)|São Paulo]].<ref name="iucn status 16 November 2021" />

==Distribution and habitat==
The black-capped piprites is distributed unevenly in the southeastern region of Brazil (south of [[Minas Gerais]] and the state of [[Rio de Janeiro (state)|Rio de Janeiro]]) and in the extreme northeast of [[Argentina]], in the province of [[Misiones]].<ref name="iucn status 16 November 2021" />

The species is found in the [[canopy (biology)|canopy]] and subcanopy around the edge of the [[Atlantic Forest]],<ref name="RR">{{cite book|last1=Ridgely|first1=Robert|last2= Tudor|first2=Guy|year=2009|chapter=Piprites pileata|page= 490 |title=Field guide to the songbirds of South America: the passerines|language=en|edition=1st|series=Mildred Wyatt-World series in ornithology|publisher=University of Texas Press|location=Austin|isbn= 978-0-292-71748-0}}</ref> especially in regions where [[Brazilian pine]]s are found.<ref name=WA>[http://wikiaves.com.br/164308&t=s&s=11400 Caneleirinho-de-chapéu-preto] en ''WikiAves''.</ref> It is found at [[altitude]]s from 500 to 2,000 meters.<ref name="iucn status 16 November 2021" />

==Description==
The adult black-capped piprites is a [[tyranid]] with a black cap and yellow beak. Its length has been measured to range from {{convert|12-12.5|cm|in}}. It has contrasting chestnut-colored upperparts, yellow legs, blackish and yellow-green [[remiges]]. In males, primary feathers are notably whitish. Females resemble males, though they are drab olive colored above rather than chestnut. The juvenile has not been described.<ref name="BoW">{{Cite BOW|citation=Snow, D. and C. J. Sharpe (2020). Black-capped Piprites (Piprites pileata), version 1.0. In Birds of the World (J. del Hoyo, A. Elliott, J. Sargatal, D. A. Christie, and E. de Juana, Editors). Cornell Lab of Ornithology, Ithaca, NY, USA. https://doi.org/10.2173/bow.bkcpip1.01}}</ref>

===Diet===
Black-capped piprites feed on small fruits and [[arthropod]]s. They have been recorded as feeding in the canopy, subcanopy and [[understorey]] of ''[[Chusquea]]'' bamboo thickets.<ref name="BoW" />

===Reproduction===
The male black-capped piprites has a display ritual it performs during the mating season. Little else is known about their reproduction.<ref name="BoW" /> The mating season takes place between September and November; nest construction has been observed in October.<ref name="iucn status 16 November 2021" />

===Vocalizations===
The species' calls include a single "''whééu''" sound, followed by a variable series of brief, softer vocalizations.<ref name="BoW" />

==Conservation==
The black-capped piprites is classified as [[Near-threatened species|near-threatened]] by the [[International Union for Conservation of Nature]], though it was classified as vulnerable in 2016, due to its small range and fragmented and declining population. In 2020, its total population was estimated at 2,800 to 22,400 mature individuals.<ref name="iucn status 16 November 2021" /> Its range is limited by the presence of Brazilian pines; recommendations have been made to remediate the declining Brazilian pine population in order to support the black-capped piprites species.<ref name=":0" />

==Systematics==
[[File:Piprites pileata 1838.jpg|thumb|left|180px|''Piprites pileata''; illustration 1838]]

=== Original description ===
The species ''P. pileatus'' was first described in 1822 by Dutch zoologist [[Coenraad Jacob Temminck]]; its type locality is [[Curitiba]], Paraná, Brazil.<ref name=":2">{{Cite web |title=Black-capped Piprites |url=https://avibase.bsc-eoc.org/species.jsp?lang=EN&avibaseid=680DC17D26470C65 |access-date=20 August 2024 |website=[[AviBase]]}}</ref>

=== Etymology ===
The generic name Piprites comes from the Greek πιπρα (''pipra'') or πιπρω (''piprō''): a small bird mentioned by [[Aristotle]] and other contemporary authors but never identified, associated with neotropical manakins in the genus ''[[Pipra]]'', and ιτης (''itēs''): "similar to", meaning in total "similar to ''Pipra''". The species name ''pileata'' comes from the Latin ''pileatus'', meaning "capped".<ref>{{Cite book |last=Jobling |first=James A. |author-link=James A. Jobling |title=The Helm dictionary of scientific bird names |publisher=Christopher Helm |year=2010 |isbn=978-1-4081-3326-2 |location=London |pages=306-308 |language=en}}</ref>

=== Taxonomy ===
The black-capped piprites is [[Monotypic taxon|monotypic]]. Its relation to the genus ''Piprites'' and other tyranids is uncertain. [[Molecular genetics|Molecular genetic]] studies in the early 2000s demonstrated relationships with [[Rhynchocyclidae]] and Tyrannidae.<ref name=":1" /> Following these studies, the family '''Pipritidae''' was proposed in 2013 with ''Piprites'' as the sole genus. The family name was adopted by Avibase<ref name=":2" /> and the Brazilian Committee for Ornithological Records,<ref>{{cite journal|access-date=20 August 2024 |author-mask=Piacentini, V. ''et al.'' |date=2015 |issn=2178-7875 |language=pt, en |last=Piacentini |number=2 |page=211 |periodical=Revista Brasileira de Ornitologia |title=Lista comentada de las aves de Brasil por el Comité Brasileño de Registros Ornitológicos |url=http://www.revbrasilornitol.com.br/BJO/article/view/1263/pdf_905 |volume=23}}<!-- auto-translated from Spanish by Module:CS1 translator --></ref> however, the [[South American Classification Committee]] (SACC) maintained the genus as ''[[incertae sedis]]'' and rejected the family name recommendation in 2016.<ref>{{cite journal|date=November 2016 |first=J.V. |language=en |last=Remsen |others=Propuesta (732) |periodical=South American Classification Committee |title=Reconocer la nueva familia Pipritidae para ''Piprites'' |url=http://www.museum.lsu.edu/~Remsen/SACCprop732.htm}}<!-- auto-translated from Spanish by Module:CS1 translator --></ref> 3 years later, the SACC approved Pipritinae as a [[subfamily]] of Tyrannidae.<ref name="SACC827">{{cite journal|date=May 2019 |first=J.V. |language=en |last=Remsen |others=Propuesta (827) |periodical=South American Classification Committee |title=Revisar la clasificación y la secuencia linear de Tyrannoidea: (A) Reconocer la nueva familia Onychorhynchidae; (B) Modificar la secuencia linear de las familias; (C) Adicionar tres subfamilias a Tyrannidae |url=http://www.museum.lsu.edu/~Remsen/SACCprop827.htm}}<!-- auto-translated from Spanish by Module:CS1 translator --></ref>

zh.extract:
黑顶娇鹟（台湾作黑頂侏儒鳥，学名：Piprites pileata），是雀形目霸鹟科的一种鸟类。


特征
黑顶娇鹟体重约15克，翼长约72毫米，喙宽度约4.3毫米，喙厚度约5毫米，嘴峰长约11.4毫米，跗蹠长约17.7毫米，尾长约50.4毫米。
黑顶娇鹟是留鸟，栖息在森林，它们是植食性动物，主要以植物的果实为食。它们分布在从巴西东南部的里约热内卢到阿根廷东北部的区域。


参考资料
